startScreenOptions.title: AN38.1-AN38.3 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN38.1-AN38.3.

interactions.label: Which anatomical feature primarily explains why early-stage glottic tumours carr

params.question: Which anatomical feature primarily explains why early-stage glottic tumours carry a better prognosis than supraglottic tumours at the same stage?

answers.text: Thicker cartilaginous walls around the glottis resist deep tumour invasion

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While cartilage can act as a barrier, the primary prognostic factor is the pattern of lymphatic drainage, not the thickness of the cartilaginous framework.

answers.text: Sparse lymphatic drainage of the true vocal cords limits nodal metastasis

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The true vocal cords have almost no lymphatic drainage, so glottic tumours rarely spread to lymph nodes. The supraglottis has rich bilateral lymphatics, leading to early and bilateral nodal metastasis.

answers.text: Glottic tumours are usually well-differentiated and slow-growing

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Histological differentiation alone does not explain the consistent prognostic difference. The key factor is the near-absence of lymphatics at the glottis.

answers.text: The glottis has a richer blood supply that mounts a stronger local immune response

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Blood supply and local immune response do not explain the prognostic difference. It is the sparse lymphatic drainage of the vocal cords that prevents early nodal spread.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key factor is lymphatic drainage. The true vocal cords have almost no lymphatics, so glottic cancers rarely spread to nodes — unlike supraglottic tumours, which drain bilaterally and early.
Exactly right. The vocal cords are virtually devoid of lymphatics, which is why glottic cancers rarely metastasise and carry an excellent prognosis when detected early.

interactions.label: Why does subglottic oedema in croup cause stridor more readily in a young child

params.question: Why does subglottic oedema in croup cause stridor more readily in a young child than in an adult?

answers.text: The child's vocal cords are shorter and vibrate at a higher frequency

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Vocal cord length affects pitch, not airway obstruction. The critical issue is where the narrowest point of the airway lies in a child.

answers.text: The child's epiglottis is more omega-shaped and obstructs airflow

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The epiglottic shape is relevant to epiglottitis, not subglottic croup. Croup affects the subglottis, below the vocal cords.

answers.text: The narrowest point of a child's airway is the subglottis, bounded by the rigid cricoid ring

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! In children, the narrowest airway segment is the subglottis at the cricoid cartilage — the only complete cartilaginous ring in the respiratory tract. It cannot expand, so even mild oedema significantly reduces the lumen, producing inspiratory stridor.

answers.text: Children have more abundant subglottic mucous glands that swell with infection

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While mucosal inflammation does occur, the key anatomical reason is that the subglottis is the narrowest part of the child's airway and is encircled by the inelastic cricoid ring.

overallFeedback.feedback: In children, the narrowest airway point is the subglottis — encircled by the rigid, non-expansile cricoid cartilage. In adults, the narrowest point is the rima glottidis. This anatomical difference explains why croup is predominantly a paediatric emergency.
Well done. The cricoid ring is rigid and cannot expand. In children, where the subglottis is the narrowest airway segment, even mild mucosal swelling causes significant airway compromise.

metadata.title: Why does subglottic oedema in croup caus

interactions.label: Why is the left recurrent laryngeal nerve more vulnerable to intrathoracic patho

params.question: Why is the left recurrent laryngeal nerve more vulnerable to intrathoracic pathology than the right?

answers.text: It has a thinner epineurium and is more easily compressed

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Both recurrent laryngeal nerves have a similar nerve sheath. The difference is in their anatomical course, not their nerve structure.

answers.text: It takes a longer intrathoracic course, looping under the aortic arch

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The left RLN loops under the aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum, giving it a much longer mediastinal course than the right nerve, which only loops under the subclavian artery. This exposes it to compression by aortic aneurysms, lung apex tumours, and an enlarged left atrium.

answers.text: It is closely related to the thoracic duct, which can enlarge in lymphatic disease

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The thoracic duct is not intimately related to the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The vulnerability is due to the nerve's long loop around the aortic arch.

answers.text: It passes directly through the left lung hilum between the bronchi

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The left RLN does not pass through the lung hilum. It loops under the aortic arch and ascends in the tracheo-oesophageal groove to reach the larynx.

overallFeedback.feedback: The left RLN loops under the aortic arch at the ligamentum arteriosum, giving it a much longer intrathoracic course than the right nerve, which loops under the subclavian artery. This longer course exposes it to compression by various mediastinal structures.
Exactly. The left RLN's long loop around the aortic arch makes it susceptible to mediastinal pathology — including aortic aneurysms, lung apex tumours, and left atrial enlargement.

metadata.title: Why is the left recurrent laryngeal nerv

interactions.label: Bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury produces a breathy voice with minimal

params.question: Bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury produces a breathy voice with minimal airway compromise.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is actually false. Bilateral RLN injury causes both cords to assume a paramedian position, obstructing the airway and producing severe stridor — it is an emergency requiring tracheostomy. A breathy voice with aspiration risk is the presentation of unilateral injury.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. Bilateral RLN injury causes both vocal cords to lie near the midline, severely narrowing the airway. The result is stridor and an airway emergency requiring urgent tracheostomy, not a breathy voice.
